reasoning: Historically, central bank liquidity swap lines see a surge in utilization toward the end of the calendar year as foreign banks (especially in Europe and Japan) face tighter private market USD funding conditions due to year-end regulatory requirements. Last year, we saw this seasonal pattern clearly: values ramped up in November (e.g., 128 in mid-Nov) and December (101), culminating in a massive spike around year-end (1,120 in early January). The recent increase from 26 to 86 on November 5 indicates that the winter ramp-up is likely underway. The FRED tool’s statistical analysis provides a combined estimate of roughly 0.46 that the upcoming values will exceed 86, based on seasonal precedents. For the first horizon (Nov 16), there is a fair chance it continues trending upward or hovers near the start-of-season averages, so I set the probability near 46%. For Dec 9, it moves closer to the peak of the year-end cycle, but as horizons extend, structural uncertainties push the estimate closer to 0.5. By Feb 7, past the year-end distortion, usage typically drops (e.g., 73 in mid-Feb last year), so the chance of remaining over 86 approaches an even split (mean-reverting, drawn to 0.5 per instructions).
